Blood pressure has been noted to have been WNL. Patient admits to not hydrating enough. Subjective This is an 90 year old Female with PMH of HTN, DM type 2, hypothyroidism, IBS, dementia, seen today for follow up visit. Patient is with her son Peter, who provides additional medical history. Patient recently underwent left reverse total shoulder arthroplasty in November 2026 with Dr. Huff and reports minimal pain and improved range of motion on her left shoulder and able to raise it about 30 degrees. Son reports patient has been doing well. She has recently noticed some lightheadedness on sudden movement, no dizziness, headache, n/v. Blood pressure has been noted to have been WNL. Patient admits to not hydrating enough. Seen by cardiology in January and was told tests done were "normal". Patient had suffered a fall in March 2023 and sustained an intertrochanteric fracture on her left femur and underwent surgery on 3/9/23 by Dr. Wallace. Patient was also found to have a subacute L3 compression fracture, interventional radiology performed kyphoplasty on 3/11/23. Reports continued slow unsteady gait, muscle weakness worse on bilateral lower extremities, and occasional left hip and knee pain. Patient would like to improve on strength and mobility, feels left leg occasionally gets stiff. Patient also reports some difficulty with fine motor movements on both hands such as buttoning her shirts. Sees podiatrist regularly, eye appointment done. No upcoming dermatology appointments. Denies any other particular complaints, including cp or sob. Plan of care discussed. Review of Systems: Constitutional: Denies weight loss, fever, chills, admits weakness and fatigue. Skin: Denies rashes, itching, skin breakdown, or ulcers. EENT: Denies visual changes; denies hearing loss, tinnitus; denies nasal congestion, runny nose or sore throat; denies difficulty swallowing. Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath, cough, or wheezing. Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, dizziness, syncope, edema. Gastrointestinal: Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain; denies changes in appetite. Admits occasional constipation. Genitourinary: Denies dysuria, urethral discharge, denies urgency and frequency, hematuria, or incontinence. Musculoskeletal: Denies joint swelling and deformities, admits muscle weakness and unsteady gait. Admits left hip and knee pain. Admits limited ROM left lower extremity. Neurologic: Denies headache, ataxia, tremor, or seizures; denies paresthesia. The patient is considered homebound as she exhibits significant functional limitations, including a slow and unsteady gait, muscle weakness in her lower extremities, and complaints of lightheadedness upon sudden movement, which collectively present a considerable fall risk and impede her ability to ambulate safely outside the home. Her dementia further compromises her safety and judgment, making unassisted excursions difficult and unsafe. Currently, she presents with a skin tear on her right forearm that has persisted for three days. Leaving the home requires a considerable and taxing effort due to these mobility impairments and cognitive deficits. Any absences would be limited to medically necessary appointments. These conditions demonstrate that patient meets Medicare criteria for homebound status and justify the need for skilled home health services, including skilled nursing for wound care and medication management, physical therapy for improving balance, strength, and overall functionality, and home health aide services to assist with personal care and activities of daily living due to her significant functional limitations.
